summary.title: Écluse
summary.description: Initial STRIDE threat model for Écluse, a supply-chain resilience proxy for package registries. The 'High Level' diagram captures the canonical deployment posture: per-caller passthrough credentials, the three-registry topology (first-party private store, public-derived mirror store, and a pull-through read endpoint), and AWS CodeArtifact reached over VPC endpoints, with the demand-driven mirror worker minting its own write token under the container role. Edge access control and storage-layer scanning are operator responsibilities and are out of scope (recorded as trust assumptions). This is an iterative sketch, refined collaboratively; the .json is the single source of truth and Threat Dragon renders it.

label.text: Operator trust zone (VPC / mesh) — access edge enforced here

data.name:
npm client
(developer / CI)
data.description: The caller. Presents its own CodeArtifact bearer token; authenticated to the proxy by the operator's access edge, not by Écluse.

data.name: Public npm registry
data.description: The public upstream. Its tarball bytes and author-supplied manifest fields are untrusted — gated by the rules engine and the integrity floor, never served blindly. The fields the registry itself asserts are trusted only to the floor that the registry is an honest operator: the per-version publish time is server-stamped (npm/PyPI/RubyGems record it server-side and it is absent from the publish document, so a publisher cannot backdate it), as is server-side integrity. A compromised or malicious registry operator forging those is below this necessary trust floor and out of scope. Queried anonymously (caller credential stripped).

threats.title: Upstream registry forges its own server-asserted metadata (e.g. a backdated publish time)
threats.description: Écluse's freshness quarantine and integrity reasoning consume fields the upstream registry asserts — notably the per-version publish time and server-side integrity. A registry that asserted forged values (a backdated time to defeat the age quarantine, or a manufactured digest) could admit content the proxy's age- and integrity-based gates would otherwise hold back.
threats.mitigation: Out of scope by trust assumption. The primary registries stamp these fields server-side — the publish time is not part of the publish document, so a publisher cannot forge it — and Écluse necessarily extends a floor of trust to the registry operator's honesty: it reads the registry's metadata, so a hostile operator is an adversary the model cannot counter (the same class as 'what if npm itself is malicious'). What is untrusted here are the tarball contents and author-supplied fields, which the rules engine and integrity floor do gate. A narrower, in-scope variant — a permissive or non-canonical upstream that accepts a client-supplied time — is a separate defence-in-depth consideration (anchoring age to Écluse's first observation), not addressed here.

data.name: AWS IMDS + STS
data.description: Instance-metadata + token endpoint. Source of the container-role credentials the worker exchanges for a CodeArtifact write token. Reached over amazonka's own client, off the guarded data-plane manager.

data.name: Écluse proxy
data.description: The request pipeline: router, parallel upstream fetch, packument merge, deny-by-default rules + integrity gate, and serve. Relays publishes. Holds forwarded caller credentials transiently.

threats.title: Forwarded caller credentials aggregated in proxy memory
threats.description: Under the canonical passthrough strategy the proxy transiently holds every caller's own CodeArtifact bearer in process memory while relaying reads and publishes. A single proxy compromise (a heap/memory dump, a log-field leak, or a malicious dependency in Écluse's own supply chain) therefore harvests the credentials of all callers in transit, not one. Passthrough distributes credential exposure across every user, where a service identity would concentrate it in one short-lived token.
threats.mitigation: Tokens are carried as a redacted type so they never reach a log field, and retention is request-scoped. Residual: a GC'd runtime cannot guarantee prompt erasure from the heap, so harden Écluse's own runtime and supply chain (the attested, reproducible image) as a first-class control. The service strategy (post-launch) is available to operators who want the smallest credential surface.

threats.title: Chokepoint exhaustion via pathological upstream payload
threats.description: Écluse is a mandatory chokepoint, so degrading its availability is itself a supply-chain attack: builds either fail or operators are tempted to bypass the gate. A hostile or compromised upstream, or a pathological public package, could return an oversized, version-flooded, or deeply-nested packument whose parsing and per-version rule evaluation exhaust CPU or memory.
threats.mitigation: Input is bounded fail-closed by body-size, version-count, and nesting-depth caps (PROXY_MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES / PROXY_MAX_VERSION_COUNT / PROXY_MAX_NESTING_DEPTH), the bounded read stopping mid-stream and the version cap failing fast during projection. The serve path is O(n log n) in version count (a Map-based merge, no super-linear blow-up), the single-flight cache coalesces concurrent misses for the same package onto one computation, the effectful advisory/CVE check is a batched local lookup rather than a per-version query, and a per-request timeout caps any single request. The residual is resource amplification, not algorithmic complexity: a near-cap document still costs real CPU and heap, and distinct-key floods (worst under a hostile or compromised upstream) bypass single-flight, so volumetric and concurrency rate-limiting is an operator-edge responsibility, as with access control.

data.name: Mirror worker
data.description: Demand-driven async worker: polls the mirror queue, back-fills the approved artifact from the public upstream, verifies bytes against dist.integrity, and publishes to the mirror store with Écluse's own minted write token.

data.name: Credential provider
data.description: Mints and refreshes the mirror-target write token under the container/task role (CodeArtifact GetAuthorizationToken), with single-flight + breaker. Écluse's only standing credential.

data.name:
Metadata cache
(public-gated only)
data.description: Shared in-proxy cache. It holds only the anonymous public (gated) origin; the per-caller private origin is never entered, under any strategy.

threats.title: Cross-client disclosure of a private package via shared cache (#115)
threats.description: A cache key carries no credential dimension, so were a private-origin document cached, one caller could warm an entry and another, differently-authorised caller could be served it without their own request being authorised upstream.
threats.mitigation: The private origin is never entered into the shared cache, under any strategy, made unrepresentable by construction; only the anonymous public-gated origin is cached, and the private origin is re-consulted per request (with the caller's own token under passthrough, or Écluse's own workload identity under service). The cache-recovering designs that would have shared a private entry (delegated-cache, memoised) are rejected by design, so no shared private cache exists to leak.

data.name: Mirror queue (SQS)
data.description: Demand-driven mirror job queue. A job is enqueued when an artifact is accepted on the tarball path. A trusted, operator-declared destination (amazonka's own client).

data.name:
Registry C:
pull-through read endpoint
data.description: The trusted private-upstream read view (PRIVATE_UPSTREAM_URL). A pull-through aggregator over the first-party store (A) and the mirror store (B); what the proxy reads.

threats.title: Undetected artifact substitution across upstreams
threats.description: The merge flags an integrity divergence when private and public copies of a version contradict on a shared digest algorithm. A weak-only or absent digest, or a flaw in how divergence is keyed, could let a substituted artifact pass undetected and be served as the trusted copy.
threats.mitigation: A public version must carry a digest meeting the integrity floor (uniform default SHA-256, hard-floored) to be admitted, and divergence is compared on each digest's asserted algorithm rather than a bucketed tag; the asserted-algorithm keying landed in #380 (closing #376). Residual: the deliberately operator-loosenable trusted-floor path, which trades strictness for availability and is the remaining way a weak digest could be accepted.

data.name:
Registry A:
private store (first-party)
data.description: First-party / internal packages, written by client npm publish (passthrough). Trusted; served unfiltered. The publication target.

data.name:
Registry B:
mirror store (public-derived)
data.description: Public packages approved by the rules and replicated by the worker. The mirror target. Kept distinct from A so first-party vs public-derived inventory stays separable.

threats.title: Registry collapse erases provenance and per-store policy
threats.description: Écluse supports 1 to 3 distinct internal registries. Collapsing the public-derived mirror store (B) into the first-party private store (A) / pull-through (C) loses the physical separation between first-party and public-derived inventory: distinct storage-level rule-sets and scanning per provenance become impossible, and post-disclosure incident scoping ('which mirrored public packages did we hold?') is muddied, weakening the arithmetic-not-forensics response.
threats.mitigation: Deploy the three-registry topology (the Golden Path): a first-party store, a public-derived mirror store, and a pull-through aggregator read endpoint, each independently governable. The trade is auditability and defence-in-depth, not a perimeter change.

data.name: npm read / publish (passthrough CodeArtifact token)
data.description: The caller's request, carrying its own CodeArtifact bearer. Crosses the operator access edge.

threats.title: Off-by-default edge auth assumes a sound network boundary
threats.description: PROXY_AUTH_TOKEN is off by default: Écluse delegates 'who may reach the proxy' to the operator's access edge (gateway / mesh / network policy). If that boundary fails, notably east-west (a compromised neighbour reaching the pod directly, bypassing an ingress-only IP allow-list), an unauthenticated caller can drive the proxy.
threats.mitigation: Compensating control: under passthrough no forwarded token means no private read and no publish, so a breach exposes only the public-gated view plus the untrusted-egress and DoS surface, never private packages. Operators must restrict both north-south and east-west access (the Golden Path documents this). The future trusted-edge-identity mode must require a verifiable edge (mTLS / shared secret), never a bare spoofable header.

data.name: read packument / tarball (caller token forwarded)
data.description: Per-request private read with the caller's own token; the upstream re-authorises each request. Never shared-cached under passthrough.

data.name: cache public-gated metadata
data.description: Read/write of the anonymous public (gated) origin only.

data.name: anonymous packument / tarball fetch (caller token stripped)
data.description: Untrusted egress to the public upstream. Attacker-influenceable target; caller credential stripped. Crosses to the public internet.

threats.title: Caller credential leak to the public upstream
threats.description: The public upstream is attacker-influenceable and must never receive a caller's credential. A failure to strip the caller token on the public fetch, or following a cross-host 3xx with the bearer attached, would disclose a live CodeArtifact token to public npm or an attacker-chosen redirect target, over the unguarded private manager.
threats.mitigation: The caller credential is stripped before every public fetch (queried anonymously). Credential-bearing requests never follow redirects: redirectCount=0 is set structurally at withToken, the single bearer-attach point (the in-use http-client does not drop Authorization on a cross-host redirect).

threats.title: SSRF via crafted identifier or upstream-declared dist.tarball
threats.description: Outbound URLs are built from client-supplied package identifiers and upstream-declared artifact locations. A traversal / encoded-slash / absolute-URL name, or a dist.tarball pointing at an internal or attacker-chosen host, could steer a fetch to an unintended target (cloud metadata, the private network).
threats.mitigation: Identifier canonicalisation with encode-on-build; an outbound host allow-list (the load-bearing control) enforced at URL build and re-applied on each redirect hop; on the untrusted manager, an internal-range block tested against the connection's pinned resolved IP (resolve-once-then-connect, so the checked address is the dialled address: the DNS-rebinding backstop); credential-bearing requests never follow redirects (redirectCount=0); and a disallow-by-default same-host dist.tarball policy. The trusted private origin is the only internal-resolving target honoured.

data.name: relay npm publish (publisher token forwarded)
data.description: First-party publish relayed to the private store with the publisher's own token. Gated by the publish-scope allow-list before any write.

threats.title: Package shadowing via first-party publish
threats.description: A publish is relayed to the private store with the publisher's own token, and the packument merge serves private versions as trusted, winning collisions over public. A compromised-CI or insider publisher who clears (or slips past) the publish-scope check could publish a name that the merge then serves as a trusted version over the public package, a dependency-confusion path through Écluse's own trust model.
threats.mitigation: The PUBLISH_SCOPES allow-list refuses any name outside the operator's scopes before any upstream write (the anti-shadowing guard); the scope match is exact on the parsed namespace, so a prefix such as @acme-evil does not satisfy an @acme allow-list. Soundness requires the authorised identity to be the written identity: the publish document's own declared name and _id (and per-version names) are validated equal to the scope-guarded URL-path name before the relay, and the write URL is built from that same canonical name, so the guarded name, the written name, and the merge collision key are one identity by construction. Residual: shadowing within an allow-listed scope and allow-precedence choices remain the operator's risk (least-privilege the publisher's target credential).

data.name: enqueue mirror job (demand-driven)
data.description: On tarball-path acceptance, a mirror job is enqueued (non-blocking).

data.name: poll jobs
data.description: The worker polls the mirror queue for demand-driven jobs.

data.name: back-fill artifact fetch (untrusted)
data.description: The worker fetches the approved artifact from the public upstream to mirror it. Untrusted egress; bytes verified against dist.integrity before publish.

data.name: request mirror-write token
data.description: The worker asks the credential provider for a mirror-target write token.

data.name: mint via container role (IMDSv2 / STS)
data.description: The credential provider exchanges container-role credentials for a CodeArtifact write token. Reaches an internal address by design.

threats.title: SSRF to the instance-metadata credential endpoint
threats.description: Container-role token minting must reach the instance-metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254 / STS). An SSRF that reached metadata could mint the worker's CodeArtifact credential.
threats.mitigation: Écluse only follows internal-resolving locations on the trusted private origin, never on a client- or upstream-influenced target, so the data plane cannot be steered at metadata; minting uses amazonka's own client off the guarded data-plane manager. Operator defence-in-depth: require IMDSv2 and set the hop limit to 1; do not block metadata outright.

data.name: publish mirrored artifact (minted write token)
data.description: The worker writes the verified, approved artifact to the mirror store with Écluse's own minted token.

threats.title: Mirror-write credential is a standing privilege over the trusted store
threats.description: The mirror worker holds Écluse's only standing self-minted credential, with write access to the mirror store (Registry B) that feeds the trusted read path. A worker compromise, or any bypass of the admission gate, could write attacker-chosen bytes into the trusted store, poisoning future reads.
threats.mitigation: Containment of this standing privilege is least-privilege IAM on the container/task role (write to Registry B only; CodeArtifact tokens bear the role's own permissions, so this is an IAM policy rather than a token-level scope), container-role minting over static credentials, and a minimal TTL (CodeArtifact caps at 12h); the publish runs with redirectCount=0 (the mint token is attached at withToken, the single bearer-attach point). The worker's dist.integrity check is anti-tamper-in-transit and anti-downgrade (it fails closed when the strongest present digest is in an uncomputable algorithm, never downgrading to a forgeable weaker one): it proves the bytes match the upstream's asserted digest, so it catches back-fill corruption but not a hostile upstream or a worker compromise. The poisoning-of-future-reads consequence is therefore bounded by admission-gate soundness (the trusted store is only as clean as what the gate admits) and the role's blast radius, not by the integrity check.

data.name: pull-through (first-party)
data.description: Registry C aggregates the first-party store A on read. Trusted, registry-level composition.

data.name: pull-through (mirrored)
data.description: Registry C aggregates the mirror store B on read. Trusted, registry-level composition.
